R Notebook

options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"))

1. Build a random forest model that can predict cell-of-origin (COO) using the training/testing cohort (70/30 training/testing split). Tune the model to select an appropriate number of trees and try value to produce the lowest out of bag(OOB) error. Report the error of the model in the trining and testing cohort(OOB, type 1, type 2 errors and a ROC curve). Discuss how well your model works on these data.

library(Biobase)
## Loading required package: BiocGenerics
## Loading required package: generics
## 
## Attaching package: 'generics'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     as.difftime, as.factor, as.ordered, intersect, is.element, setdiff,
##     setequal, union
## 
## Attaching package: 'BiocGenerics'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     IQR, mad, sd, var, xtabs
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     anyDuplicated, aperm, append, as.data.frame, basename, cbind,
##     colnames, dirname, do.call, duplicated, eval, evalq, Filter, Find,
##     get, grep, grepl, is.unsorted, lapply, Map, mapply, match, mget,
##     order, paste, pmax, pmax.int, pmin, pmin.int, Position, rank,
##     rbind, Reduce, rownames, sapply, saveRDS, table, tapply, unique,
##     unsplit, which.max, which.min
## Welcome to Bioconductor
## 
##     Vignettes contain introductory material; view with
##     'browseVignettes()'. To cite Bioconductor, see
##     'citation("Biobase")', and for packages 'citation("pkgname")'.
library(randomForest)
## randomForest 4.7-1.2
## Type rfNews() to see new features/changes/bug fixes.
## 
## Attaching package: 'randomForest'
## The following object is masked from 'package:Biobase':
## 
##     combine
## The following object is masked from 'package:BiocGenerics':
## 
##     combine
library(caret)
## Loading required package: ggplot2
## 
## Attaching package: 'ggplot2'
## The following object is masked from 'package:randomForest':
## 
##     margin
## Loading required package: lattice
## 
## Attaching package: 'caret'
## The following object is masked from 'package:generics':
## 
##     train
library(pROC)
## Type 'citation("pROC")' for a citation.
## 
## Attaching package: 'pROC'
## The following object is masked from 'package:BiocGenerics':
## 
##     var
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     cov, smooth, var
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.2.1     ✔ readr     2.2.0
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.2.2     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::combine()    masks randomForest::combine(), Biobase::combine(), BiocGenerics::combine()
## ✖ dplyr::filter()     masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()        masks stats::lag()
## ✖ purrr::lift()       masks caret::lift()
## ✖ ggplot2::margin()   masks randomForest::margin()
## ✖ ggplot2::Position() masks BiocGenerics::Position(), base::Position()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
cohort2 <- readRDS("/Users/emily/Desktop/For_LAQ1_required_data/DLBCL_cohort_2/DLBCL.cohort.2.rds")
set.seed(123)
ind <- sample(1:2, ncol(cohort2), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7,0.3))
train <- cohort2[,ind==1]
test <- cohort2[,ind==2]
train.rf <- cbind(COO = as.factor(train$COO),
                 t(exprs(train)) %>% as.data.frame())
test.rf <- cbind(COO = as.factor(test$COO),
                t(exprs(test)) %>% as.data.frame())
tune <- tuneRF(train.rf[,-1], train.rf$COO,
               ntreeTry=100,
               stepFactor=1.5,
               improve=0.01)
## mtry = 128  OOB error = 3.98% 
## Searching left ...
## mtry = 86    OOB error = 3.59% 
## 0.1 0.01 
## mtry = 58    OOB error = 4.78% 
## -0.3333333 0.01 
## Searching right ...
## mtry = 192   OOB error = 1.99% 
## 0.4444444 0.01 
## mtry = 288   OOB error = 3.98% 
## -1 0.01

best.mtry <- tune[which.min(tune[,2]),1]
rf <- randomForest(train.rf[,-1], train.rf$COO,
                   ntree=200,
                   mtry=best.mtry)
print(rf)
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(x = train.rf[, -1], y = train.rf$COO, ntree = 200,      mtry = best.mtry) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 200
## No. of variables tried at each split: 192
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 4.38%
## Confusion matrix:
##     ABC GCB class.error
## ABC 106   8  0.07017544
## GCB   3 134  0.02189781
train.pred <- predict(rf, train.rf)
confusionMatrix(train.pred, train.rf$COO)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction ABC GCB
##        ABC 114   0
##        GCB   0 137
##                                      
##                Accuracy : 1          
##                  95% CI : (0.9854, 1)
##     No Information Rate : 0.5458     
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16  
##                                      
##                   Kappa : 1          
##                                      
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : NA         
##                                      
##             Sensitivity : 1.0000     
##             Specificity : 1.0000     
##          Pos Pred Value : 1.0000     
##          Neg Pred Value : 1.0000     
##              Prevalence : 0.4542     
##          Detection Rate : 0.4542     
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.4542     
##       Balanced Accuracy : 1.0000     
##                                      
##        'Positive' Class : ABC        
## 
train.prob <- predict(rf, train.rf, type="prob")
roc.train <- roc(train.rf$COO, train.prob[,2])
## Setting levels: control = ABC, case = GCB
## Setting direction: controls < cases
plot(roc.train)

test.pred <- predict(rf, test.rf)
confusionMatrix(test.pred, test.rf$COO)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction ABC GCB
##        ABC  51   0
##        GCB   2  46
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.9798          
##                  95% CI : (0.9289, 0.9975)
##     No Information Rate : 0.5354          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : <2e-16          
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.9595          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.4795          
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.9623          
##             Specificity : 1.0000          
##          Pos Pred Value : 1.0000          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9583          
##              Prevalence : 0.5354          
##          Detection Rate : 0.5152          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.5152          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.9811          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : ABC             
## 
test.prob <- predict(rf, test.rf, type="prob")
roc.test <- roc(test.rf$COO, test.prob[,2])
## Setting levels: control = ABC, case = GCB
## Setting direction: controls < cases
plot(roc.test)

The optimal mtry value was 192, which gave the lowest OOB error during tuning (~1.99%). The final model had an OOB error of 4.38%. From the confusion matrix, the type 1 error was 7.02% and the type 2 error was 2.19%. This shows the model performs slightly better for GCB than ABC. The sensitivity was 0.9623 and specificity was 1.0000, with a balanced accuracy of 0.9811. The ROC curve shows an AUC close to 1, indicating very strong classification performance.

2. Select the 1000 most important features in the model and use these to recreate the model - Report the error of this model in the training and testing cohort(OOB, type 1, type 2 errors & ROC). Discuss whether it performs better or worse than the previous model.

imp <- importance(rf) %>% as.data.frame()
top1000 <- rownames(imp %>% arrange(-MeanDecreaseGini))[1:1000]
train.rf.1000 <- train.rf[, c("COO", top1000)]
test.rf.1000 <- test.rf[, c("COO", top1000)]
rf.1000 <- randomForest(train.rf.1000[,-1], train.rf.1000$COO,
                        ntree=200, mtry=sqrt(1000))
print(rf.1000)
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(x = train.rf.1000[, -1], y = train.rf.1000$COO,      ntree = 200, mtry = sqrt(1000)) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 200
## No. of variables tried at each split: 32
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 1.2%
## Confusion matrix:
##     ABC GCB class.error
## ABC 112   2  0.01754386
## GCB   1 136  0.00729927

This model reduced the OOB error to 1.2%, compared to 4.38% in the previous model. The type 1 error decreased to 1.75% and the type 2 error decreased to 0.73%, showing improved performance for both classes. The model performs better with fewer features, suggesting that removing less informative genes improves classification accuracy.

3. Recursively retain the model using the n most important features (where n is a reducing number of features) to find the optimum number of genes to include in the training. Report the error of the model in the training and testing cohort, including OBB, type 1, type 2 errors & ROC. Discuss the n value chosen, explaining your strategy for gene selection and describe which of the 3 models you feel best models the data.

feature_sizes <- c(1000, 500, 200, 100, 50, 20)
results <- data.frame()
for (n in feature_sizes) {
  genes <- rownames(imp %>% arrange(-MeanDecreaseGini))[1:n]
  train.sub <- train.rf[, c("COO", genes)]
  test.sub <- test.rf[, c("COO", genes)]
  model <- randomForest(train.sub[,-1], train.sub$COO,
                        ntree=150, mtry=sqrt(n))
  test.pred <- predict(model, test.sub)
  cm <- confusionMatrix(test.pred, test.sub$COO)
  acc <- cm$overall["Accuracy"]
  results <- rbind(results, data.frame(n=n, accuracy=acc))}
results

The highest accuracy (0.9899) was observed at both 200 and 50 genes.50 genes were selected as the optimal number to reduce model complexity while maintaining accuracy.When fewer than 50 genes were used, accuracy went down, which means using too few genes can remove important information.

4. Using your final optimum model, predict the cell of origin in Cohort 1 (the cohort used in the practical). Report the type 1 and type 2 errors and aROC curve in this validation cohort. Discuss whether this model works within this validation cohort and explain your reasoning.

cohort1 <- Biobase::combine(
  readRDS("/Users/emily/Desktop/For_LAQ1_required_data/DLBCL_cohort_1/DLBCL.eSet.1.rds"),
  readRDS("/Users/emily/Desktop/For_LAQ1_required_data/DLBCL_cohort_1/DLBCL.eSet.2.rds"),
  readRDS("/Users/emily/Desktop/For_LAQ1_required_data/DLBCL_cohort_1/DLBCL.eSet.3.rds"),
  readRDS("/Users/emily/Desktop/For_LAQ1_required_data/DLBCL_cohort_1/DLBCL.eSet.4.rds"))

final.genes <- rownames(imp %>% arrange(-MeanDecreaseGini))[1:100]
cohort1.rf <- cbind(COO=as.factor(cohort1$COO),
                    t(exprs(cohort1)) %>% as.data.frame())
cohort1.sub <- cohort1.rf[, c("COO", final.genes)]

pred <- predict(model, cohort1.sub)
confusionMatrix(pred, cohort1.sub$COO)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction ABC GCB
##        ABC 228 110
##        GCB  27 433
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.8283          
##                  95% CI : (0.8003, 0.8539)
##     No Information Rate : 0.6805          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.6366          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 2.457e-12       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.8941          
##             Specificity : 0.7974          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.6746          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9413          
##              Prevalence : 0.3195          
##          Detection Rate : 0.2857          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.4236          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.8458          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : ABC             
## 
prob <- predict(model, cohort1.sub, type="prob")
roc.val <- roc(cohort1.sub$COO, prob[,2])
## Setting levels: control = ABC, case = GCB
## Setting direction: controls < cases
plot(roc.val)

The sensitivity of this model was 0.8941 and specificity was 0.7974, with a balanced accuracy of 0.8458. Compared to the training and testing results, the performance decreased, indicating that the model does not generalise perfectly to independent data. The ROC curve shows an AUC of approximately 0.88–0.90, suggesting that the model still has good predictive ability. The model performs reasonably well in the validation cohort, but the drop in performance suggests some overfitting during training.

<LAQ 2>

1.Perform the unsupervised clustering analysis using non-negative matrix factorisation(NMF). Find the most appropriate number of clusters, and provide all the essential statistics and plots to support your cluster.

library(NMF)
## Loading required package: registry
## Loading required package: rngtools
## Loading required package: cluster
## NMF - BioConductor layer [OK] | Shared memory capabilities [NO: bigmemory] | Cores 2/2
##   To enable shared memory capabilities, try: install.extras('
## NMF
## ')
## 
## Attaching package: 'NMF'
## The following object is masked from 'package:generics':
## 
##     fit
exp_data <- read.table("/Users/emily/Desktop/For_LAQ2_required_data/exp_data_ESCC_90samples.txt", header=TRUE, row.names=1)
mads <- apply(exp_data,1,mad)
d_exp <- exp_data[rev(order(mads))[1:1500],]
estim.r <- nmf(d_exp, 2:6, nrun=10, seed=123456)
plot(estim.r)
## Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0.
## ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`.
## ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information.
## ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the NMF package.
##   Please report the issue to the authors.
## This warning is displayed once per session.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

consensusmap(estim.r)

res <- nmf(d_exp, 4, "brunet", seed=123456)
nmf_cluster <- predict(res)
table(nmf_cluster)
## nmf_cluster
##  1  2  3  4 
## 21 33 13 23

The consensus map at k=4 shows clearer cluster structure, while higher k values appear more unstable. At k=4, the cophenetic coefficient shows a local increase before decreasing again, and the silhouette score also improves slightly compared to k=3. I selected k=4 as the most appropriate number of clusters, and the samples were divided into four groups (21, 33, 13, and 23), suggesting a reasonable clustering structure.

2. Based on your selection of number of clusters, select and report the most significant gene signatures for each cluster (i.e., top 20 genes based on the differential expression of each cluster against all others.)

library(limma)
## 
## Attaching package: 'limma'
## The following object is masked from 'package:BiocGenerics':
## 
##     plotMA
cl <- nmf_cluster
limma_res <- list()
for (i in 1:4) {
  coi <- d_exp[, cl==i]
  rc  <- d_exp[, cl!=i]
  factors <- factor(rep(c("coi","others"),
                 c(ncol(coi), ncol(rc))))
  mm <- model.matrix(~factors-0)
  colnames(mm) <- levels(factors)
  fit <- lmFit(cbind(coi, rc), mm)
  contrast <- makeContrasts(coi-others, levels=mm)
  fit2 <- contrasts.fit(fit, contrast)
  fit2 <- eBayes(fit2)
  deg <- topTable(fit2, number=20)
  limma_res[[i]] <- deg
  print(paste("Cluster", i))
  print(head(deg, 20))}
## [1] "Cluster 1"
##              logFC   AveExpr        t      P.Value    adj.P.Val        B
## MT-ND5   11.456269 10.149411 24.39159 4.018854e-43 6.028281e-40 84.37685
## VIM       9.394897  7.735001 21.03291 7.101016e-38 5.325762e-35 73.47184
## FLNA      9.398716  8.030110 20.12768 2.290353e-36 1.145177e-33 70.28783
## AF079515  9.259422  8.246427 19.54390 2.266168e-35 8.498128e-33 68.17626
## FTL       9.781775  9.835862 18.92819 2.658691e-34 7.976073e-32 65.89875
## CD74     10.663949  8.778400 18.76182 5.213020e-34 1.303255e-31 65.27440
## AF113016  9.502621  8.961681 18.52635 1.360009e-33 2.914306e-31 64.38409
## IFI30     7.940020  6.568168 18.05043 9.647689e-33 1.808942e-30 62.56102
## TIMP1     8.032038  6.268484 17.83605 2.353718e-32 3.922864e-30 61.72940
## MALAT1    8.906858  8.331041 17.48799 1.013833e-31 1.520749e-29 60.36540
## BC078172  8.576542  6.812392 17.44512 1.214890e-31 1.656668e-29 60.19623
## DQ590013 11.210764  9.093174 17.29104 2.332308e-31 2.895095e-29 59.58599
## HLA-C     9.138293  8.545344 17.27382 2.509082e-31 2.895095e-29 59.51760

## [1] "Cluster 4"
##                  logFC   AveExpr        t      P.Value    adj.P.Val        B
## FTL          12.358903  9.835862 29.46806 4.491945e-50 6.737918e-47 98.83990

## TKT           9.135562  6.720210 26.72422 2.036474e-46 1.018237e-43 91.48984
## ABCC1         8.967267  5.594760 23.59893 6.909553e-42 2.591082e-39 82.16334
## G6PD          9.889812  6.389024 22.26317 8.091828e-40 2.427548e-37 77.83423
## SLC9A3R1      8.857296  6.762043 22.15936 1.181271e-39 2.953178e-37 77.48864
## PGD          10.240843  6.749444 21.92471 2.790239e-39 5.979084e-37 76.70257
## CBR1          9.040479  5.405450 20.97020 9.819178e-38 1.841096e-35 73.43295
## EPHX1         9.550521  6.230511 20.65138 3.301920e-37 5.503199e-35 72.31470
## MT-ND5        9.554731 10.149411 20.54323 4.995886e-37 7.493829e-35 71.93233
## TP63          8.331651  6.618768 20.36967 9.738100e-37 1.327923e-34 71.31550
## GPC1          8.599685  7.041839 19.77644 9.795369e-36 1.224421e-33 69.17689
## ABHD4         6.832542  4.540428 19.39771 4.372265e-35 5.044921e-33 67.78682
## TM4SF1        7.511104  6.258290 18.94722 2.651041e-34 2.840401e-32 66.10795
## SFN           9.763635  9.912489 18.70270 7.125191e-34 7.012138e-32 65.18505
## ATP1B1        8.221582  6.481106 18.69075 7.479614e-34 7.012138e-32 65.13971
## NQO1          9.813827  5.583336 18.56443 1.250399e-33 1.103293e-31 64.65948
## LOC100133286  7.965201  4.386001 18.48511 1.728309e-33 1.440258e-31 64.35681
## PTGR1         7.878803  4.253888 18.10433 8.264679e-33 6.524747e-31 62.89163
## AF079515      8.369967  8.246427 17.97874 1.390307e-32 1.042730e-30 62.40394

Cluster 1 : VIM, CD74, HLA-A/C/DRA, and IFI30. Cluster 2 : KRT6A, KRT17, KRT14 and SLC2A1. Cluster 3 : CSTB, CD24, ANXA1, and S100A9. Cluster 4 : G6PD, NQO1, TXN, and TKT.

3. Perform GSEA and report the most unregulated pathways for each cluster, and comment on your findings.

install.packages("BiocManager")
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  /var/folders/cy/ggjmyh4x3_j0fn2cqsk1f_800000gn/T//RtmpPempj5/downloaded_packages
BiocManager::install("clusterProfiler")
## 'getOption("repos")' replaces Bioconductor standard repositories, see
## 'help("repositories", package = "BiocManager")' for details.
## Replacement repositories:
##     CRAN: https://cloud.r-project.org
## Bioconductor version 3.22 (BiocManager 1.30.27), R 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
## Warning: package(s) not installed when version(s) same as or greater than current; use
##   `force = TRUE` to re-install: 'clusterProfiler'
## Old packages: 'biomaRt', 'devtools'
BiocManager::install("org.Hs.eg.db")
## 'getOption("repos")' replaces Bioconductor standard repositories, see
## 'help("repositories", package = "BiocManager")' for details.
## Replacement repositories:
##     CRAN: https://cloud.r-project.org
## Bioconductor version 3.22 (BiocManager 1.30.27), R 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
## Warning: package(s) not installed when version(s) same as or greater than current; use
##   `force = TRUE` to re-install: 'org.Hs.eg.db'
## Old packages: 'biomaRt', 'devtools'
BiocManager::install("enrichplot")
## 'getOption("repos")' replaces Bioconductor standard repositories, see
## 'help("repositories", package = "BiocManager")' for details.
## Replacement repositories:
##     CRAN: https://cloud.r-project.org
## Bioconductor version 3.22 (BiocManager 1.30.27), R 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
## Warning: package(s) not installed when version(s) same as or greater than current; use
##   `force = TRUE` to re-install: 'enrichplot'
## Old packages: 'biomaRt', 'devtools'
BiocManager::install("DOSE")
## 'getOption("repos")' replaces Bioconductor standard repositories, see
## 'help("repositories", package = "BiocManager")' for details.
## Replacement repositories:
##     CRAN: https://cloud.r-project.org
## Bioconductor version 3.22 (BiocManager 1.30.27), R 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
## Warning: package(s) not installed when version(s) same as or greater than current; use
##   `force = TRUE` to re-install: 'DOSE'
## Old packages: 'biomaRt', 'devtools'
library(clusterProfiler)
## Warning: package 'clusterProfiler' was built under R version 4.5.3
## 
## clusterProfiler v4.18.4 Learn more at https://yulab-smu.top/contribution-knowledge-mining/
## 
## Please cite:
## 
## Guangchuang Yu, Li-Gen Wang, Yanyan Han and Qing-Yu He.
## clusterProfiler: an R package for comparing biological themes among
## gene clusters. OMICS: A Journal of Integrative Biology. 2012,
## 16(5):284-287
## 
## Attaching package: 'clusterProfiler'
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     simplify
## The following object is masked from 'package:lattice':
## 
##     dotplot
## The following object is masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter
library(org.Hs.eg.db)
## Loading required package: AnnotationDbi
## Loading required package: stats4
## Loading required package: IRanges
## Loading required package: S4Vectors
## Warning: package 'S4Vectors' was built under R version 4.5.3
## 
## Attaching package: 'S4Vectors'
## The following object is masked from 'package:clusterProfiler':
## 
##     rename
## The following object is masked from 'package:NMF':
## 
##     nrun
## The following objects are masked from 'package:lubridate':
## 
##     second, second<-
## The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     first, rename
## The following object is masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     expand
## The following object is masked from 'package:utils':
## 
##     findMatches
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     expand.grid, I, unname
## 
## Attaching package: 'IRanges'
## The following object is masked from 'package:clusterProfiler':
## 
##     slice
## The following object is masked from 'package:lubridate':
## 
##     %within%
## The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     collapse, desc, slice
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     reduce
## 
## Attaching package: 'AnnotationDbi'
## The following object is masked from 'package:clusterProfiler':
## 
##     select
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     select
## 
gene_lists <- list()
for (i in 1:4) {
  coi <- d_exp[, cl == i]
  rc  <- d_exp[, cl != i]
  factors <- factor(rep(c("coi","others"),
                 c(ncol(coi), ncol(rc))))
  mm <- model.matrix(~factors - 0)
  colnames(mm) <- levels(factors)
  fit <- lmFit(cbind(coi, rc), mm)
  contrast <- makeContrasts(coi - others, levels = mm)
  fit2 <- contrasts.fit(fit, contrast)
  fit2 <- eBayes(fit2)
  res <- topTable(fit2, number = Inf)
  gene_list <- res$logFC
  names(gene_list) <- rownames(res)
  gene_list <- gene_list[!is.na(gene_list)]
  gene_list <- sort(gene_list, decreasing = TRUE)
  gene_lists[[i]] <- gene_list}

GSEA_res <- list()
for (i in 1:4) {
  gse <- gseGO(
    geneList = gene_lists[[i]],
    OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
    keyType = "SYMBOL",
    ont = "BP",
    pvalueCutoff = 0.05)
  GSEA_res[[i]] <- gse}
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : There were 4 pathways for which P-values were not calculated
## properly due to unbalanced (positive and negative) gene-level statistic values.
## For such pathways pval, padj, NES, log2err are set to NA. You can try to
## increase the value of the argument nPermSimple (for example set it nPermSimple
## = 10000)
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : There were 9 pathways for which P-values were not calculated
## properly due to unbalanced (positive and negative) gene-level statistic values.
## For such pathways pval, padj, NES, log2err are set to NA. You can try to
## increase the value of the argument nPermSimple (for example set it nPermSimple
## = 10000)
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some of the pathways the P-values were likely overestimated. For
## such pathways log2err is set to NA.
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : There were 36 pathways for which P-values were not calculated
## properly due to unbalanced (positive and negative) gene-level statistic values.
## For such pathways pval, padj, NES, log2err are set to NA. You can try to
## increase the value of the argument nPermSimple (for example set it nPermSimple
## = 10000)
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some of the pathways the P-values were likely overestimated. For
## such pathways log2err is set to NA.
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some pathways, in reality P-values are less than 1e-10. You can
## set the `eps` argument to zero for better estimation.
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : There were 3 pathways for which P-values were not calculated
## properly due to unbalanced (positive and negative) gene-level statistic values.
## For such pathways pval, padj, NES, log2err are set to NA. You can try to
## increase the value of the argument nPermSimple (for example set it nPermSimple
## = 10000)
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some of the pathways the P-values were likely overestimated. For
## such pathways log2err is set to NA.
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
for (i in 1:4) {
  print(paste("Cluster", i))
  print(head(as.data.frame(GSEA_res[[i]]), 10))}

## [1] "Cluster 2"

## GO:0097305                                                                                                                                                                                 AKR1C3/AKR1C2/CES1/ADH7/G6PD/SOX2/S100A8/TNC/CCND1/CLDN1/SGK1/FOS/SCNN1A/NEFL/AHR/EPS8/FGFR2
## GO:0019752                                 AKR1C3/AKR1C2/CES1/ADH7/CYP4F11/PGD/ODC1/ALDH1A1/EPHX1/CBR1/ABCC1/CYP4F3/CYP2S1/UGT1A1/GSTM4/GCLM/UCHL1/PTGR1/SLC2A1/SLC7A11/GLUL/CD74/GCLC/ME1/COL6A1/SLC6A8/FADS2/IER3/NUPR1/FADS1/GSTM1/PYCR1/NPL/FABP5/IRS2/PRODH/ASS1/CTHRC1/DDIT4/GAMT

## GO:0030216                                                                                                                           AKR1C3/KRT17/KRT6A/SFN/SPRR1B/SPRR1A/SPRR3/TP63/CSTA/ANXA1/WNT5A/ZFP36/KRT14/KRT6B/SPRR2E/KRT16/SPRR2D/CNFN/TGM3/PPL/TXNIP/KRT6C/ETV4/KRT10/S100A7
## GO:0006082                                 AKR1C3/AKR1C2/CES1/ADH7/CYP4F11/PGD/ODC1/ALDH1A1/EPHX1/CBR1/ABCC1/CYP4F3/CYP2S1/UGT1A1/GSTM4/GCLM/UCHL1/PTGR1/SLC2A1/SLC7A11/GLUL/CD74/GCLC/ME1/COL6A1/SLC6A8/FADS2/IER3/NUPR1/FADS1/GSTM1/PYCR1/NPL/FABP5/IRS2/PRODH/ASS1/CTHRC1/DDIT4/GAMT
## GO:0043436                                 AKR1C3/AKR1C2/CES1/ADH7/CYP4F11/PGD/ODC1/ALDH1A1/EPHX1/CBR1/ABCC1/CYP4F3/CYP2S1/UGT1A1/GSTM4/GCLM/UCHL1/PTGR1/SLC2A1/SLC7A11/GLUL/CD74/GCLC/ME1/COL6A1/SLC6A8/FADS2/IER3/NUPR1/FADS1/GSTM1/PYCR1/NPL/FABP5/IRS2/PRODH/ASS1/CTHRC1/DDIT4/GAMT

Cluster 1 : cell migration, cell adhesion, and locomotion-related pathways Cluster 2 : response to stimulus, defense response, and immune-related pathways Cluster 3 : keratinization and epithelial/epidermal cell differentiation pathways Cluster 4 : xenobiotic metabolism, response to toxic substances, and metabolic processes

Each cluster shows distinct biological functions, suggesting that the clustering successfully separates biologically meaningful groups.

4. Repeat the procedure 1-3 above using the consensus clustering algorithm(e.g., ConsensusClusterPlus R package) and report all the required findgings.

if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
    install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install("ConsensusClusterPlus")
## 'getOption("repos")' replaces Bioconductor standard repositories, see
## 'help("repositories", package = "BiocManager")' for details.
## Replacement repositories:
##     CRAN: https://cloud.r-project.org
## Bioconductor version 3.22 (BiocManager 1.30.27), R 4.5.2 (2025-10-31)
## Warning: package(s) not installed when version(s) same as or greater than current; use
##   `force = TRUE` to re-install: 'ConsensusClusterPlus'
## Old packages: 'biomaRt', 'devtools'
library(ConsensusClusterPlus)

consensus_res <- ConsensusClusterPlus(
  as.matrix(d_exp),
  maxK=6,
  reps=50,
  pItem=0.8,
  pFeature=1,
  clusterAlg="hc",
  distance="pearson",
  seed=123)
## end fraction

## clustered

## clustered

## clustered

## clustered

## clustered

cc_cluster <- consensus_res[[4]]$consensusClass
table(cc_cluster)
## cc_cluster
##  1  2  3  4 
## 86  2  1  1
library(limma)
group <- factor(cc_cluster)
design <- model.matrix(~0 + group)
colnames(design) <- paste0("C", levels(group))
fit <- lmFit(d_exp, design)
contrast.matrix <- makeContrasts(
  C1vsAll = C1 - (C2+C3+C4)/3,
  C2vsAll = C2 - (C1+C3+C4)/3,
  C3vsAll = C3 - (C1+C2+C4)/3,
  C4vsAll = C4 - (C1+C2+C3)/3,
  levels = design)
fit2 <- contrasts.fit(fit, contrast.matrix)
fit2 <- eBayes(fit2)
limma_cc <- list()
top20_genes <- list()
for(i in 1:4){
  res <- topTable(fit2, coef=i, number=Inf)
  limma_cc[[i]] <- res
  top20_genes[[i]] <- rownames(res)[1:20]}
top20_genes
## [[1]]
##  [1] "KRT6A"   "CLCA2"   "KRT6B"   "DSG3"    "TACSTD2" "SPRR1B"  "KRT16"  
##  [8] "KRT6C"   "GJB2"    "MPZL2"   "KRT17"   "WNT7B"   "FXYD3"   "GBP6"   
## [15] "CXCL14"  "PTPRZ1"  "SPRR1A"  "SPRR2D"  "GJB6"    "ZNF750" 
## 
## [[2]]
##  [1] "ERV3-1"  "HES2"    "TACSTD2" "FXYD3"   "KRT15"   "CXCL6"   "TMEM158"
##  [8] "PROM2"   "HORMAD1" "PVRL4"   "ALDH3B2" "ESPN"    "DEFB1"   "CXCL1"  
## [15] "TMEM63C" "MPP7"    "LRRC17"  "FGFBP1"  "BARX2"   "DGAT2"  
## 
## [[3]]
##  [1] "SHISA2"  "KRT6A"   "EMILIN1" "MRPL21"  "PEG10"   "CALML3"  "TWIST1" 
##  [8] "LEF1"    "KREMEN2" "HOXD13"  "ETV5"    "MFAP4"   "IFI27"   "NKD2"   
## [15] "WNT10A"  "TACSTD2" "HOMER2"  "SLC16A9" "ETV4"    "ITGA3"  
## 
## [[4]]
##  [1] "RAB25"    "PRSS8"    "FXYD3"    "S100A14"  "FOLR2"    "TNS4"    
##  [7] "PPP1R14C" "HBA1"     "HBB"      "CPA4"     "PRSS23"   "TACSTD2" 
## [13] "PROM2"    "VSIG4"    "KCNK1"    "HBA2"     "CLCA2"    "EPCAM"   
## [19] "LRRC17"   "MPZL2"
library(clusterProfiler)
library(org.Hs.eg.db)
gene_lists <- list()
for (i in 1:4) {
  gene_list <- limma_cc[[i]]$logFC
  names(gene_list) <- rownames(limma_cc[[i]])
  gene_list <- sort(gene_list, decreasing = TRUE)
  gene_lists[[i]] <- gene_list}
GSEA_cc <- list()
for (i in 1:4) {
  gse <- gseGO(
    geneList = gene_lists[[i]],
    OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
    keyType = "SYMBOL",
    ont = "BP",
    pvalueCutoff = 0.05)
  GSEA_cc[[i]] <- gse}
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : There were 18 pathways for which P-values were not calculated
## properly due to unbalanced (positive and negative) gene-level statistic values.
## For such pathways pval, padj, NES, log2err are set to NA. You can try to
## increase the value of the argument nPermSimple (for example set it nPermSimple
## = 10000)
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some of the pathways the P-values were likely overestimated. For
## such pathways log2err is set to NA.
## Warning in fgseaMultilevel(pathways = pathways, stats = stats, minSize =
## minSize, : For some pathways, in reality P-values are less than 1e-10. You can
## set the `eps` argument to zero for better estimation.
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
## using 'fgsea' for GSEA analysis, please cite Korotkevich et al (2019).
## preparing geneSet collections...
## GSEA analysis...
## leading edge analysis...
## done...
for (i in 1:4) {
  print(paste("Cluster", i))
  print(head(as.data.frame(GSEA_cc[[i]])))}

##            rank                   leading_edge
## GO:0070848  328 tags=38%, list=22%, signal=32%
## GO:0007167  343 tags=40%, list=23%, signal=34%
## GO:0001503  331 tags=43%, list=22%, signal=35%
## GO:0023057  333 tags=35%, list=22%, signal=31%

## GO:0002449                                                                                                                                              C1QA/C1QB/IGLL5/IGHE/C1QC/IGHG1/FCGR2A/HLA-G/FCGR3A/FCER1G/HCST/TYROBP/HLA-A/HLA-C/C1R/ULBP2/CTSC/NKG7/SECTM1/GZMB/PTPRC/HLA-F/IL1B/ICAM1
## GO:0051607                                                                                                                                                   MMP12/IFI27/ISG15/IFI6/IFITM1/BNIP3/RNASE6/IFIT1/OAS2/IFITM2/SAMHD1/IFI44L/IFIT3/MX2/PTPRC/OAS1/MICB/NLRC5/TRIM22/MX1/IL1B/GBP1/OASL
## GO:0002443                                                                                                                                                        C1QA/C1QB/IGLL5/IGHE/C1QC/IGHG1/FCGR2A/CXCL6/HLA-G/FCGR3A/FCER1G/HCST/TYROBP/HLA-A/HLA-C/RAC2/STXBP1/C1R/ULBP2/CTSC/NKG7/SECTM1

Cluster 1 is dominated by keratin genes, indicating an epithelial/keratinization state. Cluster 2 shows strong immune and inflammatory signals. Cluster 3 is characterized by WNT/TWIST genes, suggesting an EMT-like phenotype. Cluster 4 shows epithelial and metabolic features. GSEA : cluster 1 is linked to migration/adhesion, cluster 2 to immune response, cluster 3 to differentiation, and cluster 4 to metabolic processes. Overall, the clusters are stable and biologically distinct.

5. Finally, compare the clusters and cluster membership between that based on NMF and that from consensus clustering. Are these clusters similar? Are the unregulated and down regulated pathways identified from the two algorithms similar? Comment on the comparison.

table(NMF = nmf_cluster, Consensus = cc_cluster)
##    Consensus
## NMF  1  2  3  4
##   1 17  2  1  1
##   2 33  0  0  0
##   3 13  0  0  0
##   4 23  0  0  0

Most samples from NMF clusters 2, 3 and 4 were all grouped into Consensus cluster 1. The two methods do not give similar clustering results. NMF separates the samples into four distinct groups, while consensus clustering puts them into one large cluster, so it fails to clearly distinguish subtypes. The biological differences identified by NMF are likely more meaningful.

6. Can you suggest the most appropriate procedure for any unsupervised clustering analysis of a given dataset? How to generate the most robust, reliable clusters?

Unsupervised clustering shouldn’t depend on just one method. It’s better to first select variable genes to reduce noise. Then try different clustering methods and different numbers of clusters. The main point is to choose clusters that are stable, not just based on one result. After that, check if the clusters make biological sense using methods like differential expression or GSEA. Robust clustering means the results are consistent and easy to interpret biologically.
